entry.message: Sandbox evaluated community message from sandbox.user.

input.message: Is the War Room open today?

decision.reason: This is a sandbox evaluation context marked 'Do not post.' The user query asks about War Room hours, which is not supported by project memory, project guidelines, or recent context. Per instructions, I should not state live-support hours unless present in provided materials.
guidelineSnippets:
As part of the onboarding process, you will submit a quality assessment.
Please reach out to the project team in the Zoom War Room after completing the quality
assessment. This step is mandatory for you to begin tasking. The project team will need
to perform a manual review to ensure that you passed the quality assessment and to
provide access to Cursor.
Environment Setup (return to TOC
Before getting started with the project, both Cursor and Docker must be properly set up
and running. Here is a quick guide to make sure your environment is properly set up.
Cursor
● Follow this link and download the 2.6 cursor version. If you already have Cursor
installed, uninstall it and install this specific version, without this version, the models
wonʼt work.
● Even if you already have access to the Cursor Team Plan, reach out to the Zoom
War Room and ask any of the QMs for a new plan, as it must be renewed for this
project.
● Go to the Key Manager: https://cursor-intelligence-service.outlier.ai/key-manager
● Click on View API Key
● Open Cursor Settings → Models
● Disable all models, and add:
○ claude-qwen3527b-scale
○ claude-4.6-sonnet-scale
● Enable OpenAI API Key; paste in your API Key and Override Base URL with
https://cursor-intelligence-api.outlier.ai/api/v1
● Click "Verify".
● Test these models in Cursor now.
Docker
● Make sure Docker is installed and up to date. If you don't have it yet, follow this
guide.
● Ensure Docker has enough RAM and disk space to run containers smoothly.
● Clean up unused images and containers regularly to free up space and avoid build
errors.
Project Structure (return to TOC

1.2 Clone Repo & Analyze Codebase (return to TOC
First, open a new terminal and clone the given task repo using the provided repository
clone command. Rename the cloned folder as App/. After that, explore the codebase you
cloned:
● Review the code structure and architecture
● Understand dependencies and build process
● Understand the issue type you were assigned for this task Review Issue Types
Examples section in the Appendix for specific examples):
○ New feature request: Adding a new functionality not currently in the given
code.
○ Bug injection + resolution: Introducing a breaking change to working code
○ Migration: Adapting all or part of the existing code to a new
library/pattern/version without losing its original functionality.
○ Performance Optimization: Improve speed, efficiency, or resource usage with
no changes to the original functionality.
○ Maintenance or testing enhancement: Updating dependencies, tests, or tooling
Step 2 Issue Creation and Solution (return to TOC
2.1 Write Issue Statement (return to TOC
After reviewing the codebase, you will create a prompt based on the issue type that you
were assigned. The prompt should make a request to the agent based on your issue type.
It should also be complex enough that it challenges the models (Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen
3.5 27b) so that one of them fails. You can see examples of the different issue types in
Issue Types Examples section in the Appendix.
Prompt Guidelines:
● Specific enough that it is possible for the model to succeed
● Not so detailed that it gives away the solution to the model

● Should challenge models like Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen 3.5 27b.
● Simple and clear, then add complexity if the model succeeds
Prompt and Rubric Alignment:
At the end of the task, you will write a rubric that can be used to grade at a granular level
how well the model did in solving your issue. The prompt and the rubric need to be
aligned on their level of specificity. This means:
● Prompts must explicitly mention filenames, paths, functions, or modules if the rubric
criteria reference them. If the rubric lists specific paths or APIs, the prompt must also
contain those exact references.
● It is acceptable to make the prompt unrealistically specific if that is necessary to
align with the level of granularity in the rubric and guarantee that the rubric criteria
unambiguously evaluate the solution
How to build a good prompt:
The prompt is the foundation of the task. Its quality determines the quality of every
component built on top of it (test patch, golden patch, and rubrics). Treat this step
carefully.
The goal of the prompt is to realistically simulate an issue a developer would encounter
while modifying or improving a repository. Write from that perspective: a real request,
grounded in the repo, phrased the way a developer would actually phrase it.
Follow these principles:
● Write it like a real GitHub issue. The prompt should read like something a
developer would actually open in a repo focused on one problem or request. Try
not to stack multiple unrelated changes, pile on extra features, or ask for several
different things in the same prompt. One issue, one ask. If you find yourself writing
"and also," stop and split the idea or cut it.
● Be clear and concise. State the problem directly. Difficulty should come from the
realism of the problem, not from artificial complexity or volume.
● Eliminate major ambiguity. Before finalizing, reread the prompt and ask whether
two competent developers would interpret the requirements the same way. If not,
tighten the wording.
● Use explicit requirements. Every requirement must be concrete enough that it can
be verified by a test and satisfied by the gold path solution. Avoid vague language
("make it better," "improve performance") unless the success criteria are clearly
implied by context.

● Think ahead. Before writing, plan how the gold solution and tests will look. If you
cannot picture them, the prompt is not ready.
2.2 Rate the difficulty of your issue (return to TOC
Rate your issue by counting how many of the following complexity signals apply:
1. Spread: The fix changes 3 or more files.
2. Cross-module interaction: The fix affects more than one part of the project.
3. Architectural awareness: To fix it, you need to understand how the project is
organized.
4. Non-trivial logic: The fix needs decision-making in the code, not just renaming,
moving, or deleting something.
5. Implicit edge cases: The solution should also handle special cases or possible
errors.
6. Regression risk: A bad fix could accidentally break something else.
Difficulty Signals that apply
Easy 1 - 2
Medium 3 - 4
Hard 5
2.3 Create the Issue (return to TOC
Location: App/
● For all issue types except Bug Injection, the files inside app/ must remain identical
to the original repo.
● For Bug Injection, introduce the bug directly inside the app/ folder so that this
modified version represents the faulty instance state that the solution will fix.
2.3.1 Generate Base-to-Instance Patch
You must generate an additional patch called basetoinstance.patch. If the issue type is
Bug Injection, after introducing the bug, you need to capture the exact diff between the
working base state and the broken instance state
